topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the stark finality of a closing door. Dr. Alistair Vance was leaving. My advisor. The words swam before my eyes, blurring the screen until the typeface seemed to vibrate. I reread the message three times, each time the feeling of sudden, cold emptiness settled deep in my chest. I pushed my chair back so forcefully it scraped against the linoleum floor, the sound echoing unnervingly loud in the quiet library annex. My hands started to tremble, a fine, almost imperceptible vibration that made gripping my pen feel like a physical strain. I stared at the stack of annotated research papers, the carefully constructed arguments suddenly feeling flimsy and unsupported. A slow, hot flush crept up my neck, and I had to consciously take a deep, measured breath, trying to anchor myself against the sudden internal lurch.

[story 2]

I walked into the departmental common room, expecting the usual low hum of academic chatter, but the atmosphere was strangely muted. Everyone seemed to be glancing over, their expressions a mixture of polite curiosity and something akin to shared apprehension. When I finally found a seat, my knuckles were white where I gripped my lukewarm coffee mug. Every time I looked toward the door, my gaze snagged on it, anticipating a figure that would never appear. My mind was a frantic carousel, cycling through worst-case scenarios: forgotten feedback, a complete derailment of my project, the sheer, terrifying prospect of navigating this crucial final stage alone. I picked up my laptop, the screen reflecting a pale, anxious version of my own face. I tapped the keyboard aimlessly, the rhythm uneven, as if my fingers themselves were struggling to keep pace with my racing thoughts.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the office seemed too harsh, exposing every tremor in my hands. I stood by the window, watching the campus quad, but the scenery offered no solace; it was just a vast expanse of indifferent green and grey. The silence in the room was oppressive, thick enough to choke on. I kept running a hand through my hair, feeling the slight dampness of sweat against my fingertips. When I finally forced myself to look back at the desk, I saw the framed photograph of Dr. Vance—a warm, easy smile—and the contrast between that settled memory and the current reality was jarring. A tightness constricted my throat, making swallowing difficult. I felt a profound disorientation, as if the ground beneath my feet had momentarily dissolved, leaving me unsupported and adrift in an uncertain current.
